rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions; ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the surviving entries were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not a national rank. North Carolina must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to the published us-states rank order, which is known to be corrupt. Calibrated to the methodology anchors and to the states assessed at full depth on 2026-07-19: Georgia 26.9, Iowa 30.6, Arizona 36.2, Ohio 38.1, Alaska 47.5, Colorado 52.5, Michigan 53.8, Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, New York 56.9, Illinois 60.0. North Carolina at 45.0 sits between Ohio and Alaska. Composite verified by running computeCompositeFromDimensions from site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs (methodology v1.2). Verification output: {"composite":45,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. Inputs AWR 3.4, EMP 2.4, ACT 2.8, EQU 3.2, BND 2.0, ACC 3.0, SYS 3.2, INT 2.4. baseComposite 45.0; stdDev 0.458 so consistencyMult 1.0; 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0.0; final composite 45.0, band Functional. Two sensitivity cases were also run through computeCompositeFromDimensions rather than computed by hand: raising BND from 2.0 to 3.0 (if the Renew NC scope-clarity and refusal findings were remediated) returns {"composite":48.1,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}; adding 0.5 to every dimension returns {"composite":57.5,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. North Carolina therefore cannot reach the Established band on any plausible near-term correction of a single dimension.
attribution_note: Scores North Carolina state conduct only, across both political branches, since the benchmark scores the jurisdiction rather than an administration. North Carolina was under divided government for the whole 2021-2026 window: a Democratic governor (Cooper through 2024, Stein from January 2025) and a Republican General Assembly with veto-override capacity. Several scored items are legislative actions taken over a gubernatorial veto (Senate Bill 266 on 2025-07-29, House Bill 318 on 2025-07-29, Senate Bill 558 on 2026-06-24); these are scored as state conduct, and the veto is recorded as mitigating evidence within the same subdimension rather than as a separate entity. Senate Bill 382 (December 2024) is flagged as an attribution caution and is scored narrowly. It is scored under I4 Values Alignment for the documented fact that appropriations for Hurricane Helene relief were carried in the same bill as transfers of executive appointment power, which is a values-alignment fact independent of who won the underlying litigation. It is not scored as a harm to any served population, because the constitutional questions it raised are court matters rather than service-delivery outcomes. Federal policy imposed on North Carolina is excluded. The 2025 federal reconciliation law (H.R. 1) changed Medicaid eligibility rules nationally and is not scored against the state. What is scored is state discretion: the 2023 expansion statute's own trigger clause, written by the General Assembly, which ends expansion automatically if the federal match falls below 90 percent. The June 2026 federal Chemours settlement is federal action; what is scored is the state Attorney General's discretionary decision to reject it publicly and continue North Carolina's separate groundwater suit. The Helene Disaster Case Management Program is federally funded but state-administered, and the state's July 2026 decision to extend it through January 2027 is scored.
key_evidence: North Carolina erased more than $6.5 billion in medical debt for more than 2.5 million residents, announced 2025-10-13. All 99 acute-care hospitals took part. It is the first program of its kind in the United States, it required no state funds, and it beat its own forecast of $4 billion for 2 million people.

State Auditor Dave Boliek published a 506-page performance audit in November 2025 finding that the state's Hurricane Matthew and Florence housing program took an average of 936 days just to decide whether an applicant was eligible, against an 18-month goal for the whole rebuild.

The same audit found that previous program management told staff not to send ineligibility notices, while an outside contractor was paid $480 per application per month until an application was formally marked ineligible. Roughly 1,500 applications were eventually deemed ineligible.
As of 2026-07-15, nearly 22 months after Hurricane Helene, the state's Renew NC program had completed 89 homes and had 71 under construction against 7,924 active applications.

North Carolina's Healthy Opportunities Pilots cut Medicaid costs by $164 per member per month, which the state itself published on 2026-06-02. The General Assembly had already defunded the program, and services stopped on 2025-07-01.

North Carolina's prisons need 9,682 correctional officers and are short 4,703 of them, a vacancy rate near 49 percent as of December 2025. Fourteen prisons have half or more of their officer posts empty.
The General Assembly exempted itself from the state public records law in 2023 and gave every legislator the power to destroy their own records at any time.

evidence.quote: more than $6.5 billion in medical debt has been relieved for more than 2.5 million North Carolinians over the past year
evidence.claim: national-first, state-designed relief at population scale; primary support for EQ1 Universality and AB5 Reparative Action

evidence.quote: the eligibility determination taking an average of 936 days or 2.5 years
evidence.claim: core responsiveness failure; primary support for AC1 Responsiveness and E1 Affective Resonance

evidence.quote: Previous NCORR management instructed staff not to send ineligibility notifications or close cases, according to the audit
evidence.claim: families denied the information needed to seek alternatives; drives B3 Scope Clarity to 1 and B4 Refusal Ethics to 2

evidence.quote: NCORR's accounting and monitoring was so rife with errors, the state auditor could not determine the full extent of the waste
evidence.claim: independent within-state audit established a significant blind spot and triggered restructuring; supports A3 and AB1

evidence.quote: As of July 15, 2026, the Renew NC program reported 89 completed single-family homes, 71 homes under construction, and more than 390 applicants in pre-construction
evidence.claim: the delivery failure is current, not only historical; the successor program shows the same pattern

evidence.quote: North Carolina's Healthy Opportunities Pilots reduced health care costs by $164 per month for NC Medicaid beneficiaries
evidence.claim: the state measured an upstream program, proved it worked, published the proof, and had already ended it; central to S1 and I1
evidence.source: NC Department of Health and Human Services

evidence.quote: Healthy Opportunities Pilot told to prepare for program to shutter
evidence.claim: the commitment did not survive budget pressure; primary support for I1 Consistency Under Pressure at 2

evidence.quote: The North Carolina Department of Adult Correction needs 9,682 officers to fully staff all posts across the state's prisons, with a shortfall of 4,703 people, resulting in an overall vacancy rate of nearly 49 percent
evidence.claim: helping work rests on a depleted foundation; primary support for B1 and I3

evidence.quote: A sweeping new law gives every lawmaker the ability to destroy any of their records at any time
evidence.claim: one branch removed itself from verifiability, capping AB3 Transparency at 3 despite strong executive dashboards

evidence.quote: the updated local view feature on CORE-NC allows people to see how each local government is using its opioid settlement funds including payment schedules, spending plans, past spending, annual narratives, and local contacts
evidence.claim: genuine multi-party resource and data sharing with smaller local governments; primary support for S5 at 4

evidence.quote: North Carolina Supreme Court vacates nine years of Leandro school funding orders
evidence.claim: the enforcement mechanism for the state's own constitutional education duty was removed in April 2026; supports I1 and AC5

evidence.quote: House Bill 171 would ban state agencies from promoting, supporting, funding, implementing or maintaining workplace DEI, including in hirings, dedicated staff positions and offering or requiring DEI training
evidence.claim: removes the required bias-training infrastructure the E3 anchor at 3 depends on; supports E3 at 2

evidence.quote: Black women accounted for 43% of the state's pregnancy-related deaths from 2020 to 2022
evidence.claim: a measured, named, persistent disparity with reforms stalled in 2026; supports EQ3 and E3

evidence.quote: The measure includes a trigger law that brings expansion to a halt if the feds pick up anything less than 90 percent
evidence.claim: state-authored conditionality on coverage for roughly 680,000 people; caps EQ1 at 4

evidence.quote: North Carolina Attorney General Jeff Jackson called the settlement an insult to the people of eastern North Carolina
evidence.claim: a public position against a major corporate polluter carrying institutional risk; supports S4 at 3
